event: Pre-ceramic Archaic peoples (Amerindian hunter-gatherers and fishers) begin settling Bonaire and the Leeward islands.
Early Archaic-age groups leave shell middens and stone tools across Bonaire, marking the islands' first human presence.
Caquetío Arawak peoples, related to mainland Venezuela, establish ceramic-age settlements and rock art sites on Bonaire.
Caquetío communities flourish on Bonaire, painting petroglyphs and pictographs at sites such as Onima.
Christopher Columbus's second voyage reaches the region, opening the Lesser Antilles to Spanish exploration.
Spanish explorer Alonso de Ojeda sights Bonaire while charting the Venezuelan coast and the ABC islands.
Spain deports much of Bonaire's Caquetío population to Hispaniola as enslaved labor, depopulating the island.
Spain designates Bonaire a 'useless island' and turns it into a cattle and livestock ranch.
Dutch and French settlers first attempt to occupy uninhabited Saba and Saint Eustatius in the northern Leewards.
The Dutch West India Company captures Bonaire from Spain during the Eighty Years' War.
The Dutch settle Saint Eustatius (Statia), beginning Dutch control of the island.
Dutch colonists from Saint Eustatius settle Saba, establishing the island's first permanent European-led community.
The Peace of Westphalia confirms Dutch possession of the islands as Spain recognizes the independent Dutch Republic.
Saint Eustatius is captured by the English during the Second Anglo-Dutch War, beginning decades of repeated conquests.
Bonaire becomes a plantation society producing salt, maize and livestock worked by enslaved Africans under Dutch rule.
Saint Eustatius develops into a busy free-trade entrepôt as its warehouses line the lower town along Oranje Bay.
Saint Eustatius's prosperity earns it the nickname 'The Golden Rock' as a hub of Atlantic and slave trade commerce.
Honen Dalim, one of the oldest synagogues in the Americas, is built on Saint Eustatius, reflecting its Jewish merchant community.
Saint Eustatius's Fort Oranje fires the 'First Salute' to the American warship Andrew Doria, the first foreign recognition of the United States.
British Admiral George Rodney sacks Saint Eustatius in retaliation for arming American revolutionaries, looting its immense wealth.
Saint Eustatius is restored to Dutch control after brief French occupation, but its golden age of trade never fully recovers.
An enslaved-people's uprising on Bonaire and unrest across the region reflect growing resistance to Dutch slavery.
The islands are definitively returned to the Netherlands after the Napoleonic Wars and the era of British and French occupations ends.
Bonaire, Saint Eustatius, Saba and the other Dutch Caribbean islands are placed under a unified colonial administration.
The six Dutch Caribbean islands are grouped into the colony of Curaçao and Dependencies.
The Netherlands abolishes slavery in its Caribbean colonies, emancipating the enslaved people of Bonaire, Statia and Saba.
Post-emancipation Saba and Statia decline economically, with many residents emigrating and women turning to 'Saba lace' needlework.
Oil refining begins to transform the regional economy as Curaçao's Shell refinery draws labor migrants from Bonaire and Statia.
Bonaire's salt industry and aloe cultivation sustain the island as it remains a quiet dependency of Curaçao.
After the German invasion of the Netherlands, the islands fall under Allied protection and host wartime garrisons.
Bonaire hosts a wartime internment camp and an Allied airfield as the Caribbean becomes strategically important in World War II.
Saba's first motorable road, hand-built by islander Josephus Lambert Hassell, is completed after engineers said it was impossible.
The Charter for the Kingdom of the Netherlands grants the Netherlands Antilles, including the three islands, internal autonomy.
Flamingo Airport opens on Bonaire, boosting access and laying groundwork for the island's tourism.
Saba's Juancho E. Yrausquin Airport opens with one of the world's shortest commercial runways carved into the island.
Bonaire's pioneering dive-tourism industry takes off, establishing the island as a world-renowned scuba destination.
Bonaire National Marine Park is established, protecting the island's reefs and pioneering Caribbean marine conservation.
Aruba secedes from the Netherlands Antilles, prompting debate over the future of the remaining five islands.
The Saba Conservation Foundation is created and the Saba Marine Park is established to protect the island's waters.
Saba University School of Medicine opens, becoming an important institution and economic driver for the tiny island.
A referendum on Bonaire shows islanders favoring a direct tie to the Netherlands over the Netherlands Antilles.
Saba and Saint Eustatius vote in referendums on their constitutional status within the dissolving Netherlands Antilles.
The Netherlands Antilles is dissolved and Bonaire, Saint Eustatius and Saba become special municipalities of the Netherlands.
The US dollar replaces the Netherlands Antillean guilder as the official currency of the three Caribbean Netherlands islands.
Hurricane Irma devastates the northern Leeward islands, damaging Saba and Saint Eustatius as one of the strongest Atlantic storms recorded.
The Dutch government dissolves Saint Eustatius's island council and takes over its administration over governance failures.
Saint Eustatius regains partial self-government as the Netherlands begins restoring local democratic powers.
